coredata.dc:description: A novel hybrid nanostructure was synthesized by functionalizing an iron-based metal-organic framework (MOF) with carbon quantum dots (CD@MOF). The developed CD@MOF had an octahedral crystal structure and exhibited favorable antioxidant, antifungal, and antibacterial activities. MOF and CD@MOF particles are incorporated into agar/gelatin (Agar/Gel) blend polymers. The compatibility and dispersion of CD@MOF in the polymer were confirmed through FTIR, XPS, and SEM analysis. The conjugation of MOF with CDs enriched in active edges resulted in superb antioxidant (71.9 % DPPH and 100 % ABTS) and antibacterial activities. The addition of CD@MOF increased the tensile strength and gas (water vapor and oxygen) barrier and UV barrier (100 % UV blocking) properties of the film. In addition, tomato fruits were packaged with fabricated films and stored at 4 ℃ for 24 days, and quality changes were investigated. The experimental outcomes indicated the effective preservation performance of the Agar/Gel/CD@MOF films on packaged tomatoes.

head.ce:title: Synergistic effect of iron-based metal-organic framework hybridized with carbon quantum dots in agar/gelatin films for fruit preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel hybrid nanostructure was synthesized by functionalizing an iron-based metal-organic framework (MOF) with carbon quantum dots (CD@MOF). The developed CD@MOF had an octahedral crystal structure and exhibited favorable antioxidant, antifungal, and antibacterial activities. MOF and CD@MOF particles are incorporated into agar/gelatin (Agar/Gel) blend polymers. The compatibility and dispersion of CD@MOF in the polymer were confirmed through FTIR, XPS, and SEM analysis. The conjugation of MOF with CDs enriched in active edges resulted in superb antioxidant (71.9 % DPPH and 100 % ABTS) and antibacterial activities. The addition of CD@MOF increased the tensile strength and gas (water vapor and oxygen) barrier and UV barrier (100 % UV blocking) properties of the film. In addition, tomato fruits were packaged with fabricated films and stored at 4 ℃ for 24 days, and quality changes were investigated. The experimental outcomes indicated the effective preservation performance of the Agar/Gel/CD@MOF films on packaged tomatoes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Perishable agricultural products such as fruits and vegetables have a short shelf life of just a few days. Approximately 50 % of global fruit and vegetable production is lost at various stages within the food supply chain (Álvarez-Hernández et al., 2021). Tomatoes are the most grown and widely consumed vegetable crop worldwide, producing approximately 163 million tons annually. However, about 50 % of the produced tomatoes are wasted during post-harvest processing, resulting in serious economic and environmental consequences (Duguma, 2022). The main causes of post-harvest losses are associated with rot caused by the respiration of the fruit and the presence of spoilage microorganisms, mainly phytopathogenic fungi (Álvarez-Hernández et al., 2021). Packaging plays an important function in preserving fresh fruits such as cherry tomatoes and protecting them from harmful factors such as oxygen, light, water vapor and the risk of microbial contamination. Unlike traditional passive packaging, active packaging is designed to extend the shelf life of products by releasing functional compounds in a controlled manner or absorbing hazardous substances in the food surrounding environment (Jayakumar et al., 2023; Locali-Pereira et al., 2021).

Recently, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with 3D crystal structures have emerged as nanofillers in the field of active food packaging. MOFs are a category of porous crystalline nanomaterials composed of various metal ions along with organic ligands (Khan et al., 2023). Due to their cage-like structure, large surface area, and tunable functionality, they are increasingly used for the encapsulation of food-related compounds and active food packaging in terms of ethylene/oxygen absorption capacity, mechanical strength, and bioactive functionality (Hong et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2022a). Among them, an iron-based metal-organic framework (Fe-MOF) is environmentally friendly with low preparation costs (Fattahi et al., 2023). However, although the tunable internal surface properties and excellent thermal stability of Fe-MOF are advantageous for improving the physical aspects of packaging materials, their weak functional properties limit their application in the active packaging sector (Huang et al., 2021).

On the other hand, the desired functional properties of MOFs can be further improved through functionalization of MOFs. Hybridization of MOFs with carbon dots (CD/MOFs) is an ideal approach for the development of nanofillers with inherent stability and functionality (Aguilera-Sigalat & Bradshaw, 2016; Wu et al., 2020). Green carbon dots derived from natural resources have recently attracted great attention in the field of active packaging research due to their simple synthesis method, excellent antioxidant/antibacterial ability, low toxicity, and fluorescent properties (Hong et al., 2024; Moradi et al., 2023). The presence of a sp2 hybridized conjugated carbon core with a shell containing functional groups such as -COOH, -NH, and -OH in CDs confers additional functionalization capabilities (Tejwan et al., 2021). Close heterostructure or interface interactions between CDs and MOFs can improve the properties of MOFs in terms of stability and functionality without changing their morphology (Ren et al., 2019; Xia et al., 2020). The geometric configuration and large surface of the MOF provide suitable tuning capabilities for the stabilization and gas adsorption abilities of CDs (Ananthi et al., 2023). Additionally, the inorganic-organic hybrid nature of MOF nanocrystals results in strong compatibility with polymer matrices (Huang et al., 2024). The combination of CDs and a metal-organic framework exhibits a synergistic effect, so the addition of CDs@MOF to the polymer is expected to increase the mechanical strength, antibacterial properties, antioxidant performance, gas barrier ability, and UV protection effect of the composite film.

Utilizing bio-based polymers as the polymer matrix for these active packaging films is expected to be a very advantageous alternative to common synthetic polymer materials. Within the bio-based polymer category, polysaccharides and proteins are widely used because of their suitable physical and sustainable properties (Asgher et al., 2020). Gelatin is a water-soluble protein derived from animal tissues such as bones, skin, and fish skin (Riahi et al., 2023). Gelatin has excellent film-forming ability and is used to manufacture edible or biodegradable films, but it has problems such as high hydrophilicity, weak gas barrier properties, and insufficient mechanical strength (Roy et al., 2022). A blend of gelatin and polysaccharides has been proposed as an approach to compensate for the limitations of gelatin films. Previous studies have highlighted agar/gelatin mixtures as improved formulations of composite films, demonstrating compatibility and producing films with enhanced physical properties (Kim et al., 2022; Mohajer et al., 2017).

In this study, a new packaging system was developed by combining CD-doped iron-based MOF nanostructures (CD@MOF) with an agar/gelatin mixed matrix. Hybridization of CDs and Fe-MOFs to induce synergistic functional properties and the evaluation of their interaction with agar/gelatin polymer matrix was attempted for the first time. To this end, the effectiveness of CD@MOF in promoting the mechanical integrity, gas barrier, antibacterial, and antioxidant capacity of the prepared agar/gel films was evaluated. Additionally, cherry tomato packaging experiments were conducted to investigate the suitability of agar/gelatin (Agar/Gel)-based composite films for food packaging applications.

Agar and food-grade gelatin with a 200 Bloom value (type A) were obtained from Gel-Tec Co. Ltd. (Seoul, Korea). Terephthalic acid (TPA), iron(III) chloride, dimethylformamide (DMF), 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), and potassium persulfate were procured from Sigma-Aldrich were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Listeria monocytogenes (ATCC 15313), Escherichia coli O157: H7 (ATCC 43895), Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 13565), and Salmonella enterica (ATCC 14028) were obtained from the Korean Collection for Type Culture (KCTC). Penicillium chrysogenum (ATCC 22959) and Aspergillus flavus (ATCC 22546) were procured from the Korean Culture Center of Microorganisms (KCCM). The mustard seed and fresh cherry tomatoes were purchased from a local supermarket in Seoul, Korea.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots hybridized with metal-organic frameworks (CD@MOF)

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using a simple hydrothermal method (Fig. S1). For this, 1.0 g of dried mustard powder was dispersed into 45 mL of water, placed into a 100 mL capacity stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 190 °C for 8 h. After cooling, the reaction mixture was centrifuged twice at 8000 rpm for 15 min to collect the CD solution of supernatant. The solution was filtered using a Whatman membrane filter (25 mm diameter nylon filter, pore size 0.22 µm) to collect the CD solution. The CD solution was kept in a refrigerator before further testing.

The iron-based metal-organic framework was fabricated by solvothermal method according to the method of Fattahi et al. (2023). First, 1.34 g FeCl3 .6 H2O was dissolved in 10 mL DMF. Separately, 0.412 g TPA was added to 30 mL DMF. Then, the two solutions were mixed slowly and reacted in an autoclave reactor at 110 °C for 20 h. After the reaction vessel was cooled to room temperature, the orange precipitate was washed three times with ethanol by centrifugation. The obtained product was dried in a vacuum atmosphere (at 60 °C for 12 h) for further use.

Facile physical stirring was used to fabricate functional CD@MOF hybrids. The prepared carbon dots (50 mL; 6 mg/mL) and MOFs (50 mL; 3 mg/mL) solutions were mixed to keep the ratio of 2:1 (w/w), followed by stirring the solution mixture at 25 °C overnight. Then, the CD@MOF was collected by centrifuging the solution mixture at 8000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the resulting precipitate was vacuum-dried at 70 °C for 12 h to obtain the CD@MOF powders.

The gelatin solution was prepared by dissolving 2 g of gelatin powder in distilled water (100 mL) and stirring vigorously at 90 °C for 1 h. Separately, agar (2 g) and glycerol (1.2 g) were dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water by heating at 90 °C for 1 h. Then, the prepared polymer solutions were blended uniformly and stirred continuously for 60 min. The MOF and CD@MOF particles were homogeneously dispersed in distilled water using an ultrasonic probe for 20 min to ensure the uniform distribution of prepared particles in the polymer matrix. After that, 2 wt% (based on polymer) of MOF and CD@MOF were individually mixed into the above Agar/Gel solution and vigorously stirred for 20 min. The solution blend (200 mL) was poured onto a glass plate covered with a Teflon film (30 cm × 24 cm) and dried at 25 °C for 60 h. The dried films were peeled off from the plate and conditioned in a humidity chamber controlled at 25 °C and 50 % RH for 48 h before further testing. The manufactured films were designated as Agar/Gel, Agar/Gel/MOF, and Agar/Gel/CD@MOF, respectively.
Details of the characterization methods for the CD@MOF and Agar/Gel-based films are provided in the Supporting Information .

Fresh cherry tomatoes (three replicate samples) were packed in 10 cm × 10 cm sterile Agar/Gel, Agar/Gel/MOF, and Agar/Gel/CD@MOF films and sealed with polyethylene sheets. Tomatoes packaged only in polyethylene bags and in Petri dishes without packaging were used as controls. All prepared packages were stored at 4 °C for 25 days, and quality changes were investigated by sampling every 6 days to quantitatively measure weight loss, hardness, pH, and bacterial growth of stored fruits.

Weight samples were measured before and after storage. The rate of weight loss in tomatoes was determined by comparing their initial weight with their weight after storage. The firmness of cherry tomatoes was assessed through compression tests using an FR5105 texture analyzer (Taiwan Rootlon) equipped with a 30 mm cylindrical probe, and the test speed was measured at 1 mm/s. Firmness was recorded as maximum puncture force (N). Tomato juice samples were obtained by mixing 5 g of tomatoes from each test group with 45 mL of 0.1 % peptone water using a stomacher (BagMixer 400, Interscience, France). Then, the pH value of the obtained juice was recorded using a digital pH meter. Additionally, 1 mL of the diluted homogenate was plated on agar plates, and the total viable cell count (TVC) was calculated to estimate the bacterial population present in the stored test samples. The procedure was performed in triplicate, and the results were expressed as average values.

All measurements were repeated at least three times, and data were expressed as mean ± SD (standard deviation). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed, and the significance of each mean attribute value was assessed (p < 0.05) using Duncan's multiple range test using Statistical Analysis (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) software.

The morphologies of MOF and CD@MOF were observed by SEM and TEM, as shown in Fig. 1 a-d. The SEM image of the MOF crystal clearly shows the octahedral structure with an average size of 500 nm (Fig. 1 a). Due to the small size of CDs, SEM images of CD@MOF did not reveal discernible distinctions due to the limitations of SEM analysis in recording small particle sizes (Fig. 1 b). However, the CD@MOF nanostructures exhibited increased surface roughness compared to pure MOF, and several pores appeared after CD doping, which is in good agreement with previously reported results (Fattahi et al., 2023; Lin et al., 2020; Y. Zhang et al., 2021). Similarly, TEM images of pure MOFs observed octahedral crystals with smooth surfaces and sharp edges (Fig. 1 c). High-magnification TEM images of CD@MOF particles showed effective and dispersed decoration of carbon quantum dots on the surface of MOF nanostructures (Fig. 1 d), confirming the successful creation of CD@MOF hybrid structures.

The molecular structure of synthesized samples was determined by infrared spectroscopy (Fig. 1 e). The peak located at about 3200–3600 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching mode of the hydroxyl group within the MOF structure. The band at 1697 cm−1 in the MOF spectrum can be assigned to the vibration of the CO bond. The peaks at 1585 cm−1 and 1387 cm−1 are due to the asymmetrical and symmetrical vibration modes of the carboxylic group, respectively. The peak observed at 1503 cm−1 was attributed to the CC vibration of the benzene ring of MOF. The absorption bands at 1155, 1016, and 747 cm−1 are linked to the bending vibration of C-H bonds. The band at 1453 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of Fe-O, indicating the successful development of the MOF nanostructures (Fattahi et al., 2023). The characteristic peaks at 2854 to 2920 and 1275 cm−1 of CD@MOF ascribed to the C-H vibration of sp2 or sp3 hybridized carbon and C-N stretching, respectively. Furthermore, the peak intensity around 3200–3600 cm−1 (hydroxyl group) and 1697 cm−1 (carboxyl group) were intensified for the MOF samples doped with carbon dots. The observed change in the surface functional group of CD@MOF particles can be ascribed to the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, or amine groups on the surface of the synthesized CDs, validating the successful decoration of CDs on the MOF surface (Rehman et al., 2021).

The phase composition and crystallinity of the prepared samples were examined by XRD analysis (Fig. 1 f). The MOF nanostructures exhibited well-defined characteristic peaks at 2θ of approximately 8.7°, 11.7°, 17.9°, and 26.8°, which were recorded as characteristic of iron-based MOF crystals. Additionally, diffraction peaks appear at 23.7°, 34.7°, 35.9°, 40.3°, 54.3°, 62.0°, 57.5°, and 62.4°, which are attributed to the (hkl) plane of the MOF crystal at (012), (104). (110), (113), (116), (018), and (214) can be well-indexed for Fe-MOF (Fattahi et al., 2023). For CD@MOF, the presence of CD within the MOF structure is indicated by a distinct peak around 2θ = 20°, which is attributed to the turbostratic carbon phase, as previously reported (Lu et al., 2016). Additionally, the typical broad peaks associated with CDs were observed to transform into sharp peaks, which is a result of the well-dispersed CDs in the MOF during hybrid synthesis, ultimately forming a crystalline composite structure (ur Rehman et al., 2021). Moreover, other characteristic peaks retain their original patterns consistent with the MOF, confirming that the incorporation of CDs did not change the crystalline phase of the MOF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial and antioxidant activities of MOF and CD@MOF
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of food packaging materials is a key function to mitigate the risk of food spoilage caused by harmful microorganisms (Huang et al., 2024). The antimicrobial activity of the MOF and CD@MOF particles was tested against four bacteria strains (L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, E. coli, and S. enterica) employing the standard well diffusion technique and against two fungal strains (A. flavus and P. chrysogenum) using poisoned food assay ( Fig. 2 a-c). The pure MOF particles showed negligible antibacterial activity against all tested bacteria. However, the CD@MOF nanostructures showed inhibition zones of 6.6, 7.9, 2.5, and 6.3 mm against L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, E. coli, and S. enterica. The antibacterial test results are consistent with previous studies, implying that carbon dot decoration on the MOF surface can induce remarkable bactericidal activity against all Gram-positive and Gram-negative strains tested (He et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2024).

On the other hand, complete fungal growth coverage for A. flavus and P. chrysogenum strains was observed on control agar plates without functional fillers, while some antifungal activity was obtained in the group treated with pristine MOF. However, fungal growth was significantly inhibited in the group treated with CD@MOF, suggesting an enhanced antifungal effect of MOF particles synthesized with CD. Previous studies have demonstrated that metal-organic frameworks functionalized with various components have enhanced antifungal efficacy against both unicellular and multicellular fungi (Qubais Saeed et al., 2023; Tella et al., 2020).

The synergistic effect of metal-organic framework and carbon dot functional materials can confer favorable bioactive performance to CD@MOF hybrid structures. The antibacterial ability of MOF-based hybrid nanostructures (CD-MOFs) can be attributed to the following mechanisms. (i) Carbon dots rich in functional groups can easily bind to porins, peptidoglycans, and proteins in bacterial cell membranes through hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions, leading to the damage of cell wall and bio-membrane and release of intracellular constituents (Tejwan et al., 2021). (ii) The unique shape and massive structural porosity of MOFs increase the density of active edge and corner sites, facilitating interaction with microbial surfaces (Sheta et al., 2022; Tejwan et al., 2021). (iii) The presence of free metal ions in solution can promote cooperative interactions between metal ions and bonds. For example, Fe+3 ions can bind to donor bond atoms such as N-, O-, and S-, creating binding forces on the bacterial cell wall, destroying the bacterial cell wall and causing bacterial death (Sheta et al., 2022). (iv) Metal-induced reactive oxygen species (ROS) cause oxidative damage to microbial cell membranes and internal organelles such as mitochondria, nucleic acids, and proteins, ultimately leading to cell death. In particular, the efficiency of ROS (OH• and O2 •) formation of metal ions (Fe3+) can be improved by coupling it with CD. This enhancement is due to the favorable upconversion photoluminescence properties, impressive light-induced electron transport, and unique electron storage properties of CDs, which suppress electron-hole recombination (Tejwan et al., 2021). Therefore, the joint effect of hybridization of CDs and MOFs produced a very promising functional material that acts as a powerful reinforcement of packaging to eradicate harmful molds and bacteria.

The antioxidant activity of the MOF and CD@MOF composites was studied using ABTS and DPPH scavenging methods (Fig. 2 d). ABTS and DPPH compounds are stable free radicals that are susceptible to quenching by antioxidants through electron/proton transfer. The concentration-dependent trend of radical quenching ability was evident for both MOF and CD@MOF nanostructures, with improved efficiency within the concentration range of 50–60 μg/mL. The pristine MOF showed 35.4 % ABTS and 14.3 % DPPH scavenging activity at the concentration of 600 μ g/mL. However, the maximum ABTS and DPPH quenching effect for CD@MOF at a concentration of 600 μg/mL was 95.1 % and 79.9 %, respectively. The results indicated that the carbon dots induced an improved ABTS•+ and DPPH• neutralization capacity into the MOF. This acceptable antioxidant capacity of the carbon dots is due to the electron/hydrogen transfer potential, the generation of radical adducts through the sp2 carbon core and the proton transfer from the functional groups (Riahi et al., 2024b; Tejwan et al., 2021). The abundant active sites of carbon dots combined with their well-dispersed presence on the large surface of the MOF promote the migration of H+/e−, resulting in notable radical scavenging ability in CD@MOF (Khan et al., 2023b; Zhao et al., 2022).

FTIR analysis was performed to assess the surface functional groups present in the formulated films, as depicted in Fig. 3 a. The FTIR spectrum of Agar/Gel film showed all the characteristic bands of agar and gelatin. The absorption band observed at 3288 cm−1 corresponds to the O-H/N-H stretching of agar and gelatin. The absorption peaks at ∼2935 and 2871 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of C-H bonds in the alkane groups of the agar and gelatin. The characteristic peaks at 1633, 1548, and 1234 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibrations of amide I (CO), amide II (N-H), and amide III (C-N and N-H) within the gelatin structure (Riahi et al., 2023). The agar polymer exhibited the characteristic peaks at 1159 cm−1 due to the ester sulfate group, and the bands at 1038 and 927 cm−1 signify the agar’s CO stretching groups of 3,6-anhydro-D-galactose (Priyadarshi et al., 2021). In general, the peaks detected in the agar/gelatin-based films integrated with MOF and CD@MOF were very similar to those of the control Agar/Gel film, with only slight changes in peak intensity. Differences in peak intensity are likely due to physical interactions (van der Wall interactions and H-bonds) between the biopolymer and the fillers (MOF and CD@MOF).

The crystallographic properties of the Agar/Gel-based film were examined through XRD analysis (Fig. 3 b). The Agar/Gel film showed a wide peak at 2θ = ∼20°, indicating the semi-crystalline structure of agar and gelatin biopolymers. The XRD pattern of Agar/Gel/MOF and Agar/Gel/CD@MOF showed the additional peaks at 8.7°, 11.7°, 17.9°, and 26.8° related to the crystal structure of MOF, indicating a good dispersion and interaction of fillers. Similar results have been reported for biopolymers reinforced with various metal-organic framework hybrid materials (Ananthi et al., 2023; Feng et al., 2023; Pak et al., 2022).

The results of the XPS are shown in Fig. 3 (c-h), providing insights into both the surface elemental composition and the interaction between the Agar/Gel matrix and the filler components. The XPS survey scan result revealed that Agar/Gel film mainly includes carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements, while the additional elements of sulfur and iron in the Agar/Gel/CD@MOF film validate the presence of CD@MOF hybrid particles in the composite film structure (Fig. 3 c). The deconvoluted XPS spectra of C 1 s in the Agar/Gel sample demonstrated four discrete peaks ascribed to the H/C-C (283.5 eV), CC (285.1 eV), C-O (286.7 eV) and OC-O/OC ( 287.4 eV) (Fig. 3 d) (Zhang et al., 2024). However, the broadening of the peak at the binding energy of 283.5 eV in the Agar/Gel/CD@MOF film is due to the CC/C-N bonds. The O 1 s signal of both films was fitted into three peaks at 530.1, 530.9, and 532.6 eV, corresponding to the structure of OC-O, C-O, and CO/C-O-C bonds (Fig. 3 e). The high-resolution N 1 s spectra of both films exhibit the C-N-C, N-C3, and N-H bonds at three components peaks of 398.2, 399.2, and 400.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 3 f) (Khan et al., 2024). The XPS spectrum of S 2p in Agar/Gel/CD@MOF confirmed the presence of two characteristic sulfur states of -SO3-C (166.8 eV) and [SO4]2- (168.9 eV) (Fig. 3 g) (Lacey et al., 2016), due to the carbon dots in the CD@MOF.

The Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 peaks in the Fe-MOF were situated around ∼725 eV and ∼710 eV, respectively, suggesting the occurrence of the metal electron transfer sites. The peak corresponding to the Fe 2p3/2 orbital in Fig. 3 h can be further delineated into 709.7 eV (Fe2+) and 712.6 eV (Fe3+). The peaks corresponding to the Fe 2p1/2 orbitals were divided into 722.3 (Fe2+), 725.8 (Fe3+), and 728.9 (Fe3+) (Peng et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2022b).

The morphological analysis of Agar/Gel-based films is depicted in Fig. 4 a,b. The Agar/Gel film exhibited a smooth and continuously homogeneous microstructure without any pores or phase separation, suggesting favorable miscibility of agar and gelatin polymers. The photographs of Agar/MOF and Agar/Gel/CD@MOF showed a compact and smooth surface morphology, indicating that the incorporation of MOF and CD@MOF hybrid filler didn’t disrupt the homogeneity of the films. The proper distribution of fillers within the matrix without harsh agglomeration signifies their effective interaction with the polymer blend matrix and robust intermolecular connections (Subbuvel & Kavan, 2022). Furthermore, the cross-section morphology of Agar/Gel film revealed an intact structure with fine wavy clumps (Fig. 4 b). However, an intact view without any lumps or cracks was observed for both composite films, depicting the good dispersion of developed fillers in the Agar/Gel matrix. Moreover, the similar microstructure observed in Agar/Gel/MOF and Agar/Gel/CD@MOF may be attributed to the same filler concentration (2 wt%), and the presence of decorated carbon dots did not impact the effective dispersion of the developed MOF within the Agar/Gel matrix. Previous studies have documented the successful dispersion of different MOFs, including Fe-MIL 88 A (Ananthi et al., 2023), Co-MOF (Feng et al., 2023), MOF-5 (Pak et al., 2022), and Cu-MOF (Riahi et al., 2023), within various biopolymeric matrices.

The elemental composition and distribution of designed films were recorded through EDX and elemental mapping (Fig. S2). The obtained identification EDX peaks of Agar/Gel film showed only C, O, and N elements. In contrast, the presence of Fe and S elements in the EDX spectra of Agar/Gel/CD@MOF confirmed the successful inclusion of CD@MOF filler into the polymer matrix. Moreover, the uniform distribution of Fe and S elements in the elemental mapping of Agar/Gel/CD@MOF verified the appropriate dispersion of particles to the biopolymer matrix.

The UV protection and color characteristics of films play an essential role in safeguarding food items from degradation caused by light exposure, maintaining their visual appeal, and enhancing consumer confidence in the overall freshness and safety of packaged goods (Riahi et al., 2024). Fig. 4 c illustrates the visual representation of the Agar/Gel-based films, and the films' color characteristics are quantitatively detailed in Table 1. The neat Agar/Gel film appeared transparent and colorless, with an L-value of 89.98 ± 0.01. The diminished L (lightness) value and heightened a (redness) and b (yellowness) values in Agar/Gel/MOF films signified a reduction in brightness and an intensified red/yellow hue due to the incorporation of orange MOF particles. Nevertheless, the Agar/Gel/CD@MOF formulation exhibited a rise in the b value in comparison to the film containing only pure MOF, attributed to the brownish hue of the CD@MOF induced by the presence of decorated carbon dots. As a result, the total color difference (ΔE) of the developed films showed a substantial rise with the incorporation of functional particles. As depicted in Fig. 4 c, all films displayed a uniform and translucent visual aspect, suggesting the suitability of this packaging material for consumer approval. Fig. 4 d demonstrated the UV–visible light transmission spectra of the agar/gelatin-based films. The pristine Agar/Gel film showed low UV transmittance due to the intrinsic UV light absorption properties of gelatin resulting from the π-π * transition of the CO group (Khan et al., 2024). The integration of MOF particles significantly decreased transmission in the UV regions, indicating their UV absorption potential attributed to the unique electronic structure configurations of MOFs (Laurier et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2016). The addition of CD@MOF into the matrix conferred notable UV-blocking characteristics (89.6 % UV-A and 99.5 % UV-B blocking ability) to the Agar/Gel-based film (Fig. 4 e), owing to the well-documented UV light absorption capabilities of carbon dots (Barhoum et al., 2023).

Additionally, the Agar/Gel film exhibits high transmittance within the visible light region of the spectrum, affirming its considerable transparency. However, the transparency of the film was further significantly lowered by the incorporation of MOF and CD@MOF particles into the Agar/Gel film due to their light-scattering effects. The light transmittance values of the Agar/Gel-based films measured at 280 nm (T280) and 660 nm (T660) (Table 1) also confirmed that the formulated composite films possess UV-blocking attributes with a minimal loss of transparency.

Favorable mechanical properties are a key parameter for designing a robust and reliable packaging material that can safeguard food products and withstand the dynamic conditions of storage, transportation, and handling (Riahi et al., 2024a). The mechanical durability of the films was assessed through the measurement of tensile strength (TS), whereas their toughness and stiffness were analyzed through the recording of elongation at break (EB) and elastic modulus (EM), respectively, as outlined in Table 2. The thickness of the pristine Agar/Gel film was 72.0 ± 1.7 µm, and the inclusion of filler didn’t alter the film thickness significantly (p > 0.05). The TS of the Agar/Gel control film is 68.9 ± 1.5 MPa. The loading of MOF filler to the blend polymer matrix increased the TS to 81.4 ± 2.1 MPa, describing the reinforcing action of MOF agents and enhancing the film's resistance to breakage. The appropriate interfacial and intermolecular interactions between high-surface MOF particles and the Agar/Gel matrix can increase the compactness and cohesion of the film components and ensure the effective transfer of stress, improving the film strength and stiffness of film (González et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2023). Furthermore, the incorporation of the MOF component increased the stiffness of the film while the ductility of the film became weakened. The dense and complex configuration of Agar/Gel/MOF film limits the molecular mobility of the film constituents, leading to a reduction in the film's flexibility (Maroufi et al., 2023). However, after adding CD@MOF, the TS of the composite films was reduced to 75.1 ± 2.2 MPa but is still higher than in Agar/Gel film, and film flexibility was enhanced notably. The hydrophilic characteristics and functional groups of decorated carbon dots (CDs) on MOF crystals can impact the arrangement of polymer chains (Khan et al., 2024). This phenomenon can improve the alignment of polymer chains in the direction of tensile force, thereby playing a role in enhancing the elongation of the film (Xu et al., 2023).

Adequate hydrophobicity stands as a key requirement for the optimal performance of active packaging materials, ensuring the preservation of their structural integrity in moist conditions. A heightened water contact angle (WCA) directly correlates with increased surface hydrophobicity in the film (Xu et al., 2023). The Agar/Gel control film had the lowest WCA (67.9°), and the incorporation of MOF crystals distinctly enhanced the hydrophobic characteristics of the films (WCA= 78.4°) ( Fig. 5 a). The obtained result can be related to the hydrophobic feature of MOF particles along with the improved cohesion of MOF-loaded film and formed a dense multi-dimensional network structure, regulating the wettability of the formulated film (Zhang et al., 2023). However, the WCA of Agar/Gel film incorporated with CD@MOF was lower than Agar/Gel/MOF composite film (75.0°). This declination could be associated with the hydrophilic nature of carbon dots with the numerous hydrophilic groups, resulting in increased exposure of free polar groups and making them more wettable (Khan et al., 2023a).

WVP and OP assessments were conducted to evaluate and compare the water and oxygen barrier performance of the pristine Agar/Gel and functional filler blend films (Fig. 5 b). The WVP and OP of Agar/Gel film was 9.1 × 10−10 g.m/m2.s.Pa and 51.09 cm3. μ m/m2.day.atm, and the addition of MOF fillers notably reduced the WVP and OP values to 7.6 × 10−10 g.m/m2.s.Pa and 19.5 cm3· μ m/m2·day·atm, respectively. In the case of multiple sites MOF nanostructures, the overall migration of gas molecules through the biopolymer-based film can decrease by gas absorption capacity of metal-organic frameworks (Jiang et al., 2022). Moreover, studies have indicated that the presence of high surface area MOF nanostructures can occupy the pores within the film matrix, creating a compact network structure. This phenomenon increases tortuosity, effectively obstructing the diffusion of oxygen/water vapor and enhancing the film's barrier performance (Jiang et al., 2022). In addition, the intramolecular interactions (hydrogen and van der Waals bonds) between the blend polymer and MOF particles can reduce the gaps between the molecules, thereby limiting the migration of gas molecules in the film (Khezerlou et al., 2023). However, the barrier action of the film is decreased by the inclusion of CD@MOF, although it remains superior to the Agar/Gel control film. The possible reason is that the anchored CD on the MOF structure can occupy the active pore site of MOFs, reducing the surface area and their gas adsorption ability (Guo et al., 2019). The formulated composite films provided acceptable improvement in water/oxygen barrier characteristics, prolonging the food shelf life by mitigating oxidative processes and minimizing water exchange between the environment and the packaged product.

The food oxidation process involves a chain reaction, producing free radicals and potentially causing a decline in food quality. The integration of antioxidants into food packaging materials can prolong the storage life of food through the neutralization of these radicals (M. Zhang et al., 2022a). The radical neutralization efficiency of the Agar/Gel-based films is illustrated in Fig. 5 c. The pure Agar/Gel sample revealed a weak antioxidant capacity (7.3 % DPPH and 25.4 % ABTS) due to the gelatin's particular amino acids, such as glycine and proline (Bao et al., 2009). The antioxidant capability of Agar/Gel/MOF significantly increased to 29.1 % and 62.5 % for DPPH and ABTS, respectively, corresponding to the double bonds and carboxyl groups of Fe-MOF that trap the free radicals and stop the free radical reaction chain (Zhang et al., 2022a). The addition of CD-functionalized metal-organic frameworks to the Agar/Gel matrix imparted 71.9 % DPPH and 100 % ABTS radical bleaching to the film. The hydrophilic characteristics of the Agar/Gel matrix encourage the release of CD@MOF into the ABTS aqueous solution, improving the ABTS scavenging effect in comparison to DPPH radicals.

The efficacy of developed films in inhibiting bacterial growth was studied using E. coli and L. monocytogenes as model bacteria (Fig. 5d,e). The bacterial proliferation on the Agar/Gel film surface was similar to the control group (the broth without any film sample), indicating an absence of even a bacteriostatic effect for Agar/Gel film. The Agar/Gel film composited with MOF filler showed negligible antibacterial activity toward both model bacteria due to the weak antibacterial action of iron-based MOF. However, the Agar/Gel/CD@MOF reduced the E. coli proliferation to 2.4 log and completely stopped the L. monocytogenes growth after 12 h, confirming the superb antibacterial effect of synthesized hybrid CD@MOF discussed in Section 3.1.3 . The results also showed that the antibacterial effect of CD@MOF nanocomposite on L.monocyrogenes was stronger than that of E. coli. This distinction may be ascribed to the presence of an outer lipopolysaccharide membrane in the cell wall of E. coli, impeding the penetration of functional CD@MOF into the cell structure (Riahi et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Agar/Gel-based films for fruit preservation
ce:section.ce:para: The cherry tomato is one of the most popular and versatile fruits used in culinary dishes worldwide due to its plentiful nutrients and delightful taste. However, tomatoes have a short shelf life because of their high water content and susceptibility to spoilage, emphasizing the importance of proper handling and packaging to maintain freshness (Álvarez-Hernández et al., 2021). The suitability of the formulated composite films for active food packaging purposes and freshness preservation of perishable foods was examined through tomato packing ( Fig. 6). The physicochemical and microbiological aspects of tomatoes stored at different treatment groups were recorded over a 24-day storage period at 4 ℃. The visual depiction of packed fruits is shown in Fig. 6 a, revealing the different levels of fruit degradation in each group. The decay level in terms of appearance destruction and mildew spots rapidly increased for samples in control, polyethylene, and Agar/Gel packages. However, Agar/Gel/MOF composite films decreased the degradation speed of packed tomatoes. Especially, the visible decay of tomatoes packed in Agar/Gel/CD@MOF was negligible, indicating the effectiveness of the designed nanocomposite film in the storage life enhancement of tomatoes.

As shown in Fig. 6 b, weight loss increased during storage for all groups and was more intensified for the control samples than for the packed with Agar/Gel/CD@MOF composite films. The primary factor contributing to weight loss in packaged fresh fruit is the loss of water due to respiration and moisture evaporation of fruit, as well as the poor water resistance capability of the film (Aragüez et al., 2020). The shrinkage of samples in the control group and accumulated water in the polyethylene package describe the serious tomato water loss in these groups. The improved structural continuity of the developed nanocomposite films, along with the gas absorption potential of MOF particles, can hinder the movement of gases, moisture, and solutes, decreasing the metabolic processes and respiration of tomatoes.

The freshness of tomatoes in the storage process was further evaluated by monitoring the changes in firmness and pH (Fig. 6 c,d). The recorded initial firmness value for tomatoes was 4.8 kg/cm2. The firmness of tomatoes after 24 days of storage decreased to 1.1 ± 0.1, 0.8 ± 0., and 1.9 ± 0.1 kg/cm2 for the control, polyethylene, and Agar/Gel groups, respectively. However, the firmness decline of tomatoes covered with Agar/Gel/MOF and Agar/Gel/CD@MOF was significantly lower than other groups. The deterioration of cell structure induced by cell wall degrading enzymes, such as pectin methylesterase and polygalacturonase, is responsible for the softening of fruits and vegetables during post-harvest storage (Rao et al., 2011). The results suggest that the addition of CD@MOF nanostructures to a biopolymer matrix preserved the cell wall integrity of the tomatoes and mitigated their storage-induced physicochemical alterations, preventing the softening of the fruits wrapped with composite films.

Generally, the pH of tomatoes tends to rise during storage as a result of maturation and the utilization of organic acids as a substrate for respiration. A pH range of 4–4.5 is the upper acceptable pH in a tomato market (Tumwesigye et al., 2017). The pH of all tomatoes exhibited different degrees of rise as the storage time increased, where the changing trend of the Agar/Gel/CD@MOF film group was the lowest. In addition, the pH value of tomatoes stored in Agar/Gel/CD@MOF was in the edible limit range (4.14 ± 0.1), suggesting that the tomatoes covered with composite films still maintained good edible value.

Significant variations are evident in the antimicrobial effectiveness of each treatment within an in vitro model system for fresh tomatoes (Fig. 6 e). The tomatoes stored in the control, polyethylene, neat Agar/Gel, and Agar/Gel/MOF film displayed a fast microorganism growth during storage. The agar/gelatin film containing MOF and CDs fillers significantly (p<0.05) reduced the population of microorganisms on cherry tomatoes during 24 days of storage, validating the antimicrobial action of the manufactured bioactive film toward food-borne pathogens (Fig. 5 d,e).

These results strongly confirmed the improved fruit preservation performance of the engineered Agar/Gel/CD@MOF composite compared to the pure Agar/Gel films, suggesting their suitability for active food packaging to extend the life storage of post-harvest fruits.

In this study, we successfully fabricated Fe-MOF particles and efficiently loaded them with mustard-derived carbon dots. The XRD, FTIR, and TEM analyses of CD@MOF validate the successful hybridization of CD and MOFs. The incorporation of MOF and CD@MOF in the Agar/Gel blend resulted in a cohesive and uniform film, improving the hydrophobicity, mechanical, and water vapor/oxygen barrier aspects of the formulated films. Moreover, the loading of CD@MOF into the agar/gelatin biopolymer granted a UV-barrier, antioxidant, antifungal, and antibacterial effect. The assessment of various physiological (apparent view and hardness) and biochemical (pH and TVC) markers in tomato preservation revealed the efficacy of the nanocomposite film packaging treatment in effectively prolonging the storage life of tomatoes. Therefore, the current research highlights the potential application of Agar/Gel/CD@MOF nanocomposite as a promising packaging material for maintaining the freshness of fruits.

Jun Tae Kim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Gye Hwa Shin: Writing – review & editing. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing. Ajahar Khan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology. Zohreh Riahi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization.
